title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What to Do When You Miss Paying the Premium of Your Life Insurance?
Life insurance plans offer coverage against a consideration. This consideration is the premium that you pay against the coverage that the insurer provides.
In life insurance plans, there are three types of premium payments –
Single premiums, when you pay the premium in one single instalment at the start of the policy.
Limited premiums when you pay the premium for a limited tenure specified when buying the policy.
Regular premiums when you pay the premium throughout the policy tenure.
For instance, say you buy a 10-year policy. If you pay one premium when buying the policy, it is a single premium. If you pay premiums for, say, 7 years, it is a limited premium policy. On the other hand, if you pay the premium for the full 10 years, it is called a regular premium plan.
Other than a single premium plan, you must pay the premium for a specified period under the limited and regular premium plans. With each premium payment, you keep the policy in force and enjoy full coverage and bonus additions, if any. But what if you miss the premium payment on the due date? Let’s understand.
Missing the life insurance premium payment:
If you forget to pay the premium within the due date, the insurance company offers you a grace period. The grace period is the additional period for paying the missed premium.
The grace period is 30/31 days (one month) from the premium due date. For instance, if the premium due date is 15th June, you get a grace period till 15th July.
However, for policies wherein monthly premiums are paid, the grace period allowed is for 15 days. So, if you paid monthly premiums and the due date is 15th June, the grace period would end on 30th June.
How is the grace period helpful?
By extending you an extra period, the grace period allows you to pay the outstanding premium without missing out on the coverage.
During the grace period, the coverage continues. In the case of death, the outstanding premium is deducted from the claim amount, and the remaining amount is paid.
Moreover, you don’t have to pay any additional interest on the premium during the grace period. Just pay the outstanding premium, and the policy runs unaffected. Even the requirement of fresh underwriting is not required if you pay the premium within the grace period.
Missing the premium payment even during the grace period
If you do not pay the premium even during the grace period, the policy lapses. The lapse of a life insurance policy effectively means the end of the coverage. In the case of term insurance plans, if the policy lapses, you don’t get any benefit from the policy.
However, in other savings-oriented life insurance plans, like endowment and money-back plans, the policy is converted into a paid-up policy if you miss the premium payment, even in the grace period. Under a paid-up policy, the death and maturity benefits are reduced. Moreover, you don’t even get any future bonuses, if applicable. You can use the life insurance calculator to find the paid-up value that you can get based on the premiums that you have already paid against the premiums payable.
In the case of ULIPs, if you discontinue the premium within the lock-in period of five years, your premiums are transferred to the discontinued fund. There, the premiums remain invested till the lock-in period is over and earn limited returns. Once the lock-in period is over, the discontinued fund amount is refunded, and the policy is terminated.
However, if you discontinue the premium payment after the lock-in period, the invested premium remains in the same fund. You can enjoy the returns from the chosen fund.
Difference between lapse and termination:
Lapse of a life insurance policy does not mean the end of the road. You can revive your policy during the revival period by paying the outstanding premium and interest thereon. Once revived, you can continue enjoying the full coverage under the plan.
Lapse and termination mean completely different things. This is because a lapsed policy can be revived. However, when you terminate, the policy stops, and you get a surrender value.
Furthermore, if you do not revive a lapsed policy and the policy has acquired a paid-up value, you can continue the coverage till maturity. The policy will run, albeit with a reduced benefit. This reduced benefit will be paid on early death or maturity.
So, the basic differences between a lapse and a termination are as follows –
The bottom line
The full benefit of an insurance policy can be unlocked only if you pay the life insurance premiums duly and fully. Insurance companies allow an additional grace period if you forget the premium payment. So, make use of the grace period to enjoy uninterrupted coverage. Even if your policy has lapsed, you can pay the outstanding premium and revive the policy during the revival period. This will restore the coverage and allow you to continue enjoying the full benefits of life insurance.
